item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What I like about this is that initially you think this could bring about a CK Class Restructuring or something. The moment I understood the effect I thought. "O5-1! POTUS!" However, the price is never hinted at, so the limited use of the item makes sense. I also can't help wondering what prices have been paid. Has someone literally sold their grandma to be with somebody?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if it works on fictional people or characters, though it likely doesn't since the abductors have to kidnap real people to deliver them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This seems like an extended story to the SCP called "The Click Anywhere Computer".

How exactly?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This seems like an extended story to the SCP called "The Click Anywhere Computer". Perhaps you could mention that?
I did like it though. Upvoted for originality and good writing style.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one, "damn feds", that cracked me up!

Since the kidnappers are able to be killed and there was a successful abduction in a foundation facility, maybe an autopsy revealing anomalous anatomy or something? Just an idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: so, what happened if the foundation entered "the creator of scp 1243" in the name box? id love to see the results of a test like that

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: and they didnt question the crime syndicate for where they got the url? >.<

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the first paragraph (discounting the one-sentence one at the beginning) of the Description should be broken up somewhere. I'm not going to read through that otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. Well done. +1

item.title: Re: Done and done!
item.content:encoded: Great to know :D If anyone else needs a picture for their article, tell 'em to talk to me!

item.title: Re: Done and done!
item.content:encoded: Good work. It's almost exactly how I pictured it in my head.

item.title: Done and done!
item.content:encoded:
Picture with caption added! As I said in the description, feel free to revert if necessary. Let me know what you think!

EDIT: Derp, forgot to add the counter. Lemme just fix that up real quick…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I made up that MTF, actually. They're the ones who run interference on local law enforcement.

"Whatever happened with that giant talking alligator-thing?" "Eh, the damn feds came in and took it away from me. Some bullshit about jurisdiction."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the Ransom Note SCP does it perfectly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On it! :D I'll let you know when I've come up with something, and I'll add it once I've gotten the OK to do so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That might be nice. I considered doing it myself but it's been a while since I used HTML.

I didn't make up that MTF, by the way. It was already on the list of MTFs and it just seemed appropriate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it—I got a little chuckle out of the name of the MTF.

Also, to the OP: if you'd like me to — but I make no time guarantees, 'cos I'm kinda busy with life-stuff right now — I can try to make you a picture for this. Doesn't seem like it would be that hard to do, if you're interested. I can be pretty handy with these things :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can think of an easy way to do that. For whatever reason the only payment they will request from the Foundation is an SCP

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: True enough, that's a fair point. I still think the potential benefits massively outweigh the risks though, and that there are conceivable ways to minimize the risks. Maybe have a test log of something that scared them away from the idea of exploiting this and lead to the decision to stop testing? Seeing some negative consequences to using this thing would definitely help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, it says in the containment procedures that they don't want to use it because they don't know how it gathers data and they don't want to risk a containment breach.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty cool, but the fact that this incredibly useful too is available for Foundation use makes it hard to think that anyone could effectively oppose them. Why not use this thing to abduct the directors of MC&D, or Chaos Insurgency members? It just messes with my suspension of disbelief when something comes up that's incredibly powerful and useful and the Foundation doesn't use it for their own ends; I've just come to expect that kind of bastard behavior, like with SCP-596 for example.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"…the location where maintenance of SCP-1243 was being performed…."

Should that be "the location where research into SCP-1243" or similar? If the Foundation doesn't have containment of SCP-1243, I don't see how the word "maintenance" could possibly apply.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It would need to be a 3/1243 then, eh? I was just thinking in terms of writing, not actual site mechanics, mea culpa.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just noticed this, and I'm not sure it's a good idea.
Quoting Security Clearance Levels page

LEVEL 4: Command-level personnel; permitted access to all non-Keter level SCP objects.

Given that, 4/1243 is rather redundant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That actually gave me a really good idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man I want to use this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really don't like this for some reason—just the idea of being able to magically abduct people is too much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I still feel like this should still be more of a matter for law enforcement than the Foundation. It's a little more interesting than the original draft was, but it's still just an organized crime syndicate with a website and some guys what fight good.

Is there any lasting effect on the kidnapping victims? All the article says is that they "appear" in the vicinity of the ordering person - one assumes they could just simply run away, tell the police they'd been kidnapped, and the guy who placed the order would be arrested.

I think this has promise, but i'm withholding my vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So that's how they really found Hussain and Bin Laden…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has a nice creepypasta vibe to it. Upvoted.

One suggestion. You could trim this down:

Only personnel with a security clearance of 4 or higher who have been assigned to the maintenance of SCP-1243

By just making the clearance 4/1243.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, you managed to do that allright - it's the reason I upvoted it in the end.

Now , to be honest, if you have some humanoid figures do it, having it not fail no matter what happens, is , in my opinion, bad form - it's exactly the same as having them disappear, just looking different. . You started well enough with them being possible to dismember.. .consider having it such that if they get murderkilled during the retrieval attempt, additional fee is charged to the one who made the request - replacement for them.
What sort of fee.. .well, that's up to you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll keep all this in mind. I really wanted the horror factor here to be the human basterdry of those who would use a service like this. I went with the mysterious humanoid thing because I was told having normal humans doing the kidnapping didn't make it feel anomalous enough and just having the target disappear felt like a cop out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not bad for that reason.
Consider.

Instances of SCP-1243-2 are [DATA EXPUNGED] which appear in the vicinity of individuals "purchased" using SCP-1243-1, for the purpose of abducting such individuals. Subjects vary greatly in appearance, though they are usually described as vaguely humanoid.

He ends up telling us they are vaguely humanoid, and then follows to tell of their damage resistance.
That… sort of leaves me wondering why is there an expungement at all since if he had defined what they are in the original document, the next sentence is redundant, (Think of it with your filler - little green men of vaguely humanoid appearance) and if he hadn't…. then I can't quite imagine anything of importance that could have been expunged there.

Zen and the art of data redacted is good thing to read on this topic

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whenever I want to avoid EXPUNGEMENT, I just use extremely vague wording that prevents people from reading it as something utterly ridiculous

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well originally I had guys in ski masks. Someone in the discussion said that just made it feel like an abnormally effective criminal organization.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Instances of SCP-1243-2 are [ little green men ] which appear in the vicinity of individuals "purchased"

If I can do this, its a bad expungement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You missed "Incident XXX" at the bottom.

While this is a decent idea, you spend far too much time digging into the technical tidbits, and don't get to the point fast enough. I, for one, prefer to explain what the function of an object is immediately, and then go into detail afterwards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you are unsure how to tag an article, feel free to leave it untagged and I or someone else well versed in tag-fu will take care of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please read the Attribute Tag Guide, abduction isn't a tag.
